title: Meniscus (anatomy)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Fibrocartilaginous part of a bone joint

For other uses, see [Meniscus (disambiguation)](/wiki/Meniscus_(disambiguation) "Meniscus (disambiguation)").

| Meniscus | |
| --- | --- |
| Head of right tibia seen from above, showing menisci and attachments of ligaments | |
| Left knee-joint from behind, showing interior ligaments | |
| Details | |
| Identifiers | |
| [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") | *meniscus* |
| [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") | *[μηνίσκος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BC%CE%B7%CE%BD%CE%AF%CF%83%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:μηνίσκος") ("meniskos")* |
| [MeSH](/wiki/Medical_Subject_Headings "Medical Subject Headings") | [D000072600](https://meshb.nlm.nih.gov/record/ui?ui=D000072600) |
| [TA98](/wiki/Terminologia_Anatomica "Terminologia Anatomica") | [A03.0.00.033](https://ifaa.unifr.ch/Public/EntryPage/TA98%20Tree/Entity%20TA98%20EN/03.0.00.033%20Entity%20TA98%20EN.htm) |
| [TA2](/wiki/Terminologia_Anatomica "Terminologia Anatomica") | [1544](https://ta2viewer.openanatomy.org/?id=1544) |
| [Anatomical terminology](/wiki/Anatomical_terminology "Anatomical terminology") [[edit on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q846928 "d:Q846928")] | |

A **meniscus** (pl.: **menisci** or **meniscuses**) is a crescent-shaped [fibrocartilaginous](/wiki/Fibrocartilage "Fibrocartilage") [anatomical](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") structure that, in contrast to an [articular disc](/wiki/Articular_disc "Articular disc"), only partly divides a [joint cavity](/wiki/Synovial_joint#Structure "Synovial joint").[[1]](#cite_note-Platzer-208-1) In [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), menisci are present in the [knee](/wiki/Knee "Knee"), [wrist](/wiki/Wrist "Wrist"), [acromioclavicular](/wiki/Acromioclavicular_joint "Acromioclavicular joint"), [sternoclavicular](/wiki/Sternoclavicular_joint "Sternoclavicular joint"), and [temporomandibular joints](/wiki/Temporomandibular_joint "Temporomandibular joint").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Generally, the term "meniscus" is used to refer to the [cartilage](/wiki/Cartilage "Cartilage") of the [knee](/wiki/Knee "Knee"), either to the [lateral](/wiki/Lateral_meniscus "Lateral meniscus") or [medial meniscus](/wiki/Medial_meniscus "Medial meniscus"). Both are [cartilaginous](/wiki/Cartilaginous#Fibrocartilage "Cartilaginous") tissues that provide structural integrity to the knee when it undergoes [tension](/wiki/Tension_(mechanics) "Tension (mechanics)") and [torsion](/wiki/Torsion_(mechanics) "Torsion (mechanics)").[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

The menisci are also known as "semi-lunar" cartilages, referring to their half-moon, crescent shape.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term meniscus derives from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") [μηνίσκος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BC%CE%B7%CE%BD%CE%AF%CF%83%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:μηνίσκος") *meniskos*, meaning "crescent", and was first used in English around 1690.[[5]](#cite_note-oed-5) The word was used in reference to a [lens](/wiki/Lens "Lens") that is [concave](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/concave "wiktionary:concave") on one side and [convex](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/convex "wiktionary:convex") on the opposite side.[[5]](#cite_note-oed-5)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

The menisci of the knee are two pads of [fibrocartilaginous](/wiki/Cartilaginous#Fibrocartilage "Cartilaginous") tissue covering about 70% of the articular surface, serving to disperse friction and absorb weight in the [knee](/wiki/Knee "Knee") joint between the lower leg ([tibia](/wiki/Tibia "Tibia")) and the thigh ([femur](/wiki/Femur "Femur")).[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) They are concave on the top and flat on the bottom, articulating with and attached to the [tibial plateau](/wiki/Tibia#Condyles_of_tibia "Tibia"), and are thicker laterally.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

### Attachments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Attachments")]

The menisci are attached to the small depressions ([fossae](/wiki/Fossa_(anatomy) "Fossa (anatomy)")) between the [condyles](/wiki/Condyle_(anatomy) "Condyle (anatomy)") of the tibia (intercondyloid fossa), and towards the center from the rim of the bone by *insertional* ligaments.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

The outer edges of the medial meniscus are attached to the [joint capsule](/wiki/Joint_capsule "Joint capsule") and [medial collateral ligament](/wiki/Medial_collateral_ligament "Medial collateral ligament").[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) Ligamental attachments to the tibial plateau also exist from the anterior and posterior horns of the menisci, and from the anterior edges of the menisci by the transverse intermeniscal ligament.[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) The posterior horn of the lateral meniscus connects via insertional ligaments to the femur and the [popliteus](/wiki/Popliteus_muscle "Popliteus muscle") tendon.[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

### Vascular supply

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Vascular supply")]

The meniscus has a limited peripheral blood supply from the medial, lateral, and middle geniculate arteries (branches of the [popliteal artery](/wiki/Popliteal_artery "Popliteal artery")) which perfuse the inferior and superior aspects of each meniscus.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3) The blood flow courses from the periphery (outside) toward the center, decreases with age, and is mostly absent by adulthood, impeding recovery from injury.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

[Capillaries](/wiki/Capillary "Capillary") arising from these arteries originate along the periphery of the menisci.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3) Each meniscus is also nourished by [synovial fluid](/wiki/Synovial_fluid "Synovial fluid") via diffusion or mechanical pumping, such as during joint motion.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Function")]

The menisci serve diverse [biomechanical](/wiki/Biomechanics "Biomechanics") functions, participating in load transmission and shock absorption to stabilize the knee, nutrition, joint lubrication, and [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception").[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

The menisci mainly act to disperse the weight of the body and reduce friction during movement.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3) Since the condyles of the femur and tibia meet at one point (which changes during [flexion](/wiki/Flexion "Flexion") and [extension](/wiki/Human_anatomical_terms#Types_of_movement "Human anatomical terms")), the menisci spread the load of the body's weight.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

### Injury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Injury")]

Main article: [Tear of meniscus](/wiki/Tear_of_meniscus "Tear of meniscus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/MenisectomyScar1980.jpg/250px-MenisectomyScar1980.jpg)](/wiki/File:MenisectomyScar1980.jpg)

Scar from partial left meniscectomy in 1980 (c. 30 years before photo); more recent surgery leaves smaller scars.

In [sports](/wiki/Sport "Sport") and [orthopedics](/wiki/Orthopedic_surgery "Orthopedic surgery"), people sometimes speak of "torn cartilage" and will actually be referring to an injury to one of the menisci.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) There are two general types of meniscus injuries: acute tears which are often the result of trauma or a sports injury, and chronic or wear-and-tear type tears. Acute tears have many different shapes (vertical, horizontal, radial, oblique, complex) and sizes.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3) Injuries to the menisci are a cause of substantial dysfunction of the knee and leg in normal walking and in sports participation.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

Meniscus injuries are often treated with surgical repair depending upon the person's age as they rarely heal on their own.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) Chronic tears are treated symptomatically: physical therapy with or without the addition of injections and anti-inflammatory medications.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3) If the tear causes continued pain, swelling, or knee dysfunction, then the tear can be removed or repaired surgically.[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

The [unhappy triad](/wiki/Unhappy_triad "Unhappy triad") is a set of commonly co-occurring knee injuries which includes injury to the medial or lateral meniscus, [anterior cruciate ligament](/wiki/Anterior_cruciate_ligament "Anterior cruciate ligament"), and [medial collateral ligament](/wiki/Medial_collateral_ligament "Medial collateral ligament").[[6]](#cite_note-ferr-6)

### Magnetic resonance imaging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Magnetic resonance imaging")]

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a noninvasive diagnostic procedure used to evaluate the anatomical state of a meniscus, typically following a rotating knee injury.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4) MRI is widely accepted as an optimal imaging tool because of its clarity for visualizing soft tissue in contrast to bone.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)

On cross-section with MRI, the normal meniscus appears with a uniform, dark gray-like appearance, whereas a meniscal tear gives a darker signal within the meniscus.[[3]](#cite_note-fox-3)[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Conservative management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Conservative management")]

[Conservative management](/wiki/Conservative_management "Conservative management") is often considered first for a smaller or chronic tear that does not appear to require [arthroscopic](/wiki/Arthroscopy "Arthroscopy") surgery, although arthroscopy may not provide a more satisfactory outcome than exercise therapy.[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)[[8]](#cite_note-siem-8) [Physical therapy](/wiki/Physical_therapy "Physical therapy") is used both in conservative management for a minor injury and after surgery.[[4]](#cite_note-raj-4)

### Surgical treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meniscus_(anatomy)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Surgical treatment")]

Two surgeries of the meniscus are most common. Depending on the type and location of the tear, the person's age, and physician's preference, injured menisci are usually either repaired or removed, in part or completely (meniscectomy).[[9]](#cite_note-sihv-9) Each has its advantages and disadvantages. Many studies show the meniscus serves a purpose and therefore doctors will attempt to repair it when possible. However, the meniscus has poor blood supply, and, therefore, healing can be difficult. Traditionally it was thought that if there is no chance of healing, then it is best to remove the damaged and non-functional meniscus, although at least one study has shown that there is little significance if a meniscectomy is done for a degenerative tear.[[9]](#cite_note-sihv-9) However, resuming high-intensity activities may be impossible without surgery as the tear may cause the knee to lock.

A 2017 [clinical practice guideline](/wiki/Medical_guideline "Medical guideline") strongly recommends against surgery in nearly all people with degenerative knee disease.[[8]](#cite_note-siem-8)
